title: Modeling strained Cd$_3$As$_2$ thin films and their behavior in magnetic fields
authors: M. Smith, A. A. Burkov, P. P. Orth, I. Martin, Victor L. Quito
abstract: We present a systematic analysis of the behavior of thin films of Cd$_3$As$_2$ under different strain profiles and in magnetic fields. In each case, we construct effective $k \cdot p$ models by considering the reduction of symmetry and all constraints imposed by the remaining symmetries. Our analysis naturally describes both in-plane biaxial and uniaxial strain. Biaxial strain is expected to preserve in-plane $C_4$ rotational symmetry while breaking inversion, allowing for a description in terms of the $4mm$ point group. Uniaxial strain, on the other hand, breaks $C_4$ symmetry. For this case, we consider two scenarios: one preserving inversion, described by the $mmm$ group, and one breaking it, leading to $2mm$ symmetry. After deriving the models, we examine the effects of out-of-plane magnetic fields, identifying two possible microscopic mechanisms that can account for the experimental results reported in Ahadi et al. (2025). Importantly, our analysis proposes a new method for differentiating between them. By incorporating the effects of multiple subbands along the confinement direction, we show that the opening of a gap in the lowest Landau level requires either reducing the symmetry down to $2mm$, breaking both inversion and $C_4$ rotations, or a topological transition of the band structure due to strain-induced band renormalization. Furthermore, we demonstrate that a two-dimensional Dirac semimetal phase can be induced by sufficiently large in-plane magnetic fields. This phase is highly sensitive to different strain profiles, with band touchings occurring when the field is applied perpendicular to preserved mirror planes, serving as a powerful probe of the material's strain profile.
summary: This theoretical work models strained Cd3As2 thin films using k.p theory to understand their electronic response in magnetic fields. It systematically analyzes how strain dictates the crystal symmetry, which in turn governs whether Landau level crossings are gapped or if a 2D Dirac semimetal phase can emerge. The findings provide powerful diagnostic tools for characterizing topological properties in strained quantum materials.
main_problem: The study systematically analyzes how strain profiles and external magnetic fields affect the electronic band structure of strained Cd3As2 thin films.
main_result: The analysis shows that while symmetry breaking can gap out Landau Level crossings, a 2D Dirac semimetal phase can be induced by large in-plane magnetic fields, which is highly sensitive to strain orientation.
method: Effective k.p models are constructed based on group theory analysis of symmetry reduction under biaxial or uniaxial strain, followed by perturbative calculations involving subband coupling and Landau level quantization.
model_or_system: The system is Cd3As2 thin films modeled using effective Hamiltonian Hamiltonians derived from symmetry constraints ($4/mmm 	o 4mm$, $mmm 	o 2mm$). The analysis incorporates strain effects ($\delta L/L$) and magnetic fields ($\mathbf{B}$).
key_observables: Gap opening in the lowest Landau level (LLL), emergence of a two-dimensional Dirac semimetal phase, and the critical field dependence ($B_c$) for band crossings.
important_parameters: Strain profiles (biaxial vs. uniaxial), magnetic field strength ($\mathbf{B}$), film thickness ($L$), and symmetry point groups ($4mm$, $mmm$, $2mm$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on constructing effective low-energy k.p models based on underlying point group symmetries, and uses perturbative methods for subband coupling.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate strain profiles and the resulting symmetry reduction of the crystal point group (e.g., $4/mmm$ to $4mm$). Spectra show how LLL crossings behave under different strains and magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by establishing the general Hamiltonian, systematically analyzing the effect of biaxial and uniaxial strain on symmetry ($C_4$, inversion), deriving effective Hamiltonians for reduced symmetries, and finally examining the consequences of out-of-plane and in-plane magnetic fields on Landau level crossings.
why_interesting: The detailed coupling between crystal symmetry breaking (strain) and topological electronic properties (Dirac semimetals) is highly relevant to understanding novel quantum materials behavior under external stimuli.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Different strain profiles on a thin film Cd3As2 sample: in-plane biaxial strain (left), which preserves C4 ro- tations and C4-breaking uniaxial strain (right). (b) Group theory analysis of the effects of the strain on the sample, list- ing different point groups and the symmetries that are bro- ken. (c) Possible explanations for the experimental results of Ref. [1]. The first possibility is that strain renormalizes band- structure parameters and uninverts the bands at the Γ-point, trivializing the bands. The LLL crossing in magnetic field is then no longer protected and is gapped out. The second possibility is that strain lowers the symmetry by breaking C4 and/or inversion...

FIG. 2. Effects of strain in a thin film with thickness L = 19 nm. The k · p model parameters are A = 111.6 meV nm, M0 = 28.2 meV, Mz = 207.2 meV nm2, and Mxy = 133.2 meV nm2. In all cases, we explicitly include three subbands and three LLs in the diagonalization, and we have explicitly checked that our conclusions are unchanged when using more. The top panels show all bands in the energy window from −35 to 35 meV, while the bottom panels show a zoom from −5 to 5 meV. Without strain, the levels cross at B ≈27 T (dashed lines in lower panels). (a) Biaxial strain with 4mm point symmetry that preserves C4 rotations and breaks inversion, showing that the LLL crossing remains. We use Ds = Dp =...

FIG. 3. Dependence of the critical field Bc, where the cross- ing of LLLs occurs, as a function of Az. The result (dots) is obtained from exact diagonalization considering three sub- bands. For clarity, we kept all the parameters identical to the unstrained case, and set δA = 0 to pinpoint the effects of the Az term. The curve for small Az corresponds to a quadratic fit motivated by the perturbative result in Eq. (41). The crit- ical field vanishes for Az = 53 meV nm, and for even larger values, no crossing will occur as the respective subbands have uninverted and become trivial.
